text: Large language models, or LLMs, are advanced computer programs that can understand and generate human-like text. They work by predicting the most likely next word in a sentence based on patterns they have learned. To do this, they are trained on vast amounts of text data, including books, articles, and websites, which teaches them grammar, facts, and reasoning. When using an LLM, users often practice prompt engineering—carefully crafting questions or instructions to get better responses. These models have impressive capabilities, such as writing essays, answering questions, or translating languages. However, they also have limits: they may produce incorrect information, lack real understanding, and can be biased by their training data.
